pathways.label: Set-aside after a Suspended Imposition of Sentence (AS 12.55.085)
pathways.summary: A court may grant a Suspended Imposition of Sentence (SIS) for many misdemeanors and some lower-level felonies, typically for younger or first-time offenders. On successful completion of probation, the court may set aside the conviction and issue a certificate; the conviction then “ceases to have legal effect” for many purposes. It is NOT expungement. The record is not sealed or destroyed; a set-aside conviction can still appear on background checks, can be used as a prior for sentence enhancement, and can support license denials. Certain offenses are excluded from SIS / set-aside under AS 12.55.085(f) (see Section 3). 'Defendant received a…
pathways.sourceRef: pathways:78-118; section:381-387; section:78-118; corpus-unit:30; corpus-unit:106; section:56-77; section:119-151; section:152-186
ruleClauses: A court may grant a Suspended Imposition of Sentence (SIS) for many misdemeanors and some lower-level felonies, typically for younger or first-time offenders.
On successful completion of probation, the court may set aside the conviction and issue a certificate; the conviction then “ceases to have legal effect” for many purposes.

It is NOT expungement. The record is not sealed or destroyed; a set-aside conviction can still appear on background checks, can be used as a prior for sentence enhancement, and can support license denials.
Certain offenses are excluded from SIS / set-aside under AS 12.55.085(f) (see Section 3).
'Defendant received a Suspended Imposition of Sentence and has
successfully completed probation. Defendant moves the Court to
SET ASIDE the conviction under AS 12.55.085(e) and to issue a
certificate.' (NOTE: this does NOT seal or expunge; the record
remains and may appear on background checks.)
There is no general expungement. The four real mechanisms, by situation:
Set-aside after Suspended Imposition of Sentence (AS 12.55.085)
Sealing for mistaken identity / false accusation (AS 12.62.180)
A person may ask the agency that maintains the record to SEAL information that, beyond a reasonable doubt, resulted from mistaken identity or false accusation.
This is a high bar and a narrow remedy — it is not a general path for clearing accurate records.
A person whose information is sealed under this section may deny the existence of the information and of the arrest, charge, conviction, or sentence.
Confidentiality of acquittals / dismissals (AS 22.35.030, Admin. Rule 40)

The court system may NOT publish on the public Courtview website a criminal case if 60 days have passed and the person was acquitted of all charges, all charges were dismissed (not as part of a Rule 11 plea in another case), some were acquitted and the rest dismissed, or all were dismissed after a suspended entry of judgment (AS 12.55.078).

Removal is automatic, but cases sometimes slip through — Form TF-810 asks the court to exclude such a case; TF-805 removes just the person's name; TF-800 asks a judge to seal a still-public case or document.
This makes the record non-public on Courtview; it does not destroy the underlying record.
Juvenile records (AS 47.12.300) and the pardon backstop

Most juvenile records are sealed within 30 days of the 18th birthday or the court's release of jurisdiction (whichever is later); juveniles charged as adults generally seal five years after completing the sentence.
For an ordinary adult conviction that fits none of the above, the only avenue is a governor's pardon (executive clemency), which forgives but does not erase.
Lead with honesty: Alaska has no general expungement. Identify which narrow mechanism fits — SIS
set-aside, mistaken-identity sealing, Courtview non-publication, or juvenile sealing — and explain what
Never describe an SIS set-aside as “expungement”; it leaves a record that can still surface on
background checks and as a sentencing prior.

Defendant moves the Court to SET ASIDE the conviction under AS 12.55.085(e) and to issue a certificate.' (NOTE: this does NOT seal or expunge; the record remains and may appear on background checks.)

Most juvenile records are sealed within 30 days of the 18th birthday or the court's release of jurisdiction (whichever is later); juveniles charged as adults generally seal five years after completing the sentence.

Mechanism Trigger / waiting period Authority SIS set-aside On successful completion of the SIS probation AS 12.55.085(e) period (clean for the term, often ≈ 1 year+) Mistaken-identity sealing No fixed wait; proof beyond a reasonable doubt AS 12.62.180(b) Courtview non-publication 60 days after acquittal or dismissal AS 22.35.030 (acquittal / dismissal) Suspended entry of judgment On completion; dismissal w/o conviction AS 12.55.078 Juvenile sealing 30 days after 18th birthday / release of AS 47.12.300 jurisdiction; 5 years if charged as adult Two timing points matter.
AGENT GUIDANCE For acquittals/dismissals, the relief should be automatic at 60 days — have the user check Courtview first and only file TF-810 if the case is still showing.
exclusionRules: Certain offenses are excluded from SIS / set-aside under AS 12.55.085(f) (see Section 3).
The biggest limitation is structural: no general expungement exists, so an ordinary accurate adult conviction
generally cannot be cleared at all. Within the narrow mechanisms, additional exclusions apply.
Excluded from SIS / set-aside (AS 12.55.085(f))
Serious and violent offenses — e.g., murder and other homicide offenses, and offenses where an SIS is statutorily barred.
Certain crimes involving domestic violence and other offenses specifically listed in AS 12.55.085(f).
Offenses with a mandatory minimum sentence that cannot be suspended.
Set-aside is not erasure. Even when granted, the conviction remains on the record, can be a sentencing prior (AS 12.55.155(c)(8)), and may support licensing denials.
Sealing (12.62.180) is essentially limited to mistaken identity / false accusation proven beyond a reasonable doubt — not a route for clearing valid records.
Courtview non-publication only hides the case from the public website — law enforcement and agencies retain the record, and it is limited to acquittals/dismissals.
Suspended entry of judgment (12.55.078) is typically once per lifetime and has its own excluded-offense list.
Out-of-state and federal records are not reachable by Alaska's mechanisms.
The SIS / set-aside exclusion list (AS 12.55.085(f)) and the suspended-entry-of-judgment
exclusions (AS 12.55.078(f)) are specific and have been amended; the reform bills (HB 34, HB 203)
would, if enacted, add genuine expungement/destruction of set-aside records and change this
Screen SIS eligibility against AS 12.55.085(f) — homicide, sex offenses, and certain DV and
Be explicit about the ceiling: for a valid adult conviction that isn't SIS-eligible and isn't a mistaken-identity
case, Alaska currently offers no clearing beyond a governor's pardon.

Sealing for mistaken identity / false accusation (AS 12.62.180)
A person may ask the agency that maintains the record to SEAL information that, beyond a reasonable doubt, resulted from mistaken identity or false accusation.
This is a high bar and a narrow remedy — it is not a general path for clearing accurate records.

sourceSections.title: Sample filings and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Statutory shorthand used throughout

AS 12.55.085 Suspended Imposition of Sentence (SIS) and SET-ASIDE of a conviction

AS 12.55.085(f) Offenses for which an SIS / set-aside is NOT available

AS 12.55.078 Suspended ENTRY of judgment (no conviction results) — once per lifetime

AS 12.62.180 SEALING of criminal justice information (mistaken identity / false accusation)

AS 22.35.030 Acquittal / dismissal records kept off the public Courtview website

Admin. Rule 40 Court rule governing what appears on the public Courtview index

AS 47.12.300 Sealing of JUVENILE records

Form TF-810 Request to exclude a case from Courtview (Admin. Rule 40 / AS 22.35.030)

Journey v. State 895 P.2d 955 (Alaska 1995) — set-aside is NOT expungement

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules (what relief exists)
sourceSections.text:
There is no general expungement. The four real mechanisms, by situation:

Mechanism 1 — Set-aside after Suspended Imposition of Sentence (AS 12.55.085)
• A court may grant a Suspended Imposition of Sentence (SIS) for many misdemeanors and some lower-level
felonies, typically for younger or first-time offenders.
• On successful completion of probation, the court may set aside the conviction and issue a certificate; the
conviction then “ceases to have legal effect” for many purposes.
• It is NOT expungement. The record is not sealed or destroyed; a set-aside conviction can still appear on
background checks, can be used as a prior for sentence enhancement, and can support license denials.
• Certain offenses are excluded from SIS / set-aside under AS 12.55.085(f) (see Section 3).

Mechanism 2 — Sealing for mistaken identity / false accusation (AS 12.62.180)
• A person may ask the agency that maintains the record to SEAL information that, beyond a reasonable
doubt, resulted from mistaken identity or false accusation.
• This is a high bar and a narrow remedy — it is not a general path for clearing accurate records.
• A person whose information is sealed under this section may deny the existence of the information and of the
arrest, charge, conviction, or sentence.

Mechanism 3 — Confidentiality of acquittals / dismissals (AS 22.35.030, Admin. Rule 40)
• The court system may NOT publish on the public Courtview website a criminal case if 60 days have passed
and the person was acquitted of all charges, all charges were dismissed (not as part of a Rule 11 plea in
another case), some were acquitted and the rest dismissed, or all were dismissed after a suspended entry of
judgment (AS 12.55.078).
• Removal is automatic, but cases sometimes slip through — Form TF-810 asks the court to exclude such a
case; TF-805 removes just the person's name; TF-800 asks a judge to seal a still-public case or document.
• This makes the record non-public on Courtview; it does not destroy the underlying record.

Mechanism 4 — Juvenile records (AS 47.12.300) and the pardon backstop
• Most juvenile records are sealed within 30 days of the 18th birthday or the court's release of jurisdiction
(whichever is later); juveniles charged as adults generally seal five years after completing the sentence.
• For an ordinary adult conviction that fits none of the above, the only avenue is a governor's pardon
(executive clemency), which forgives but does not erase.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with honesty: Alaska has no general expungement. Identify which narrow mechanism fits — SIS
set-aside, mistaken-identity sealing, Courtview non-publication, or juvenile sealing — and explain what
each does and does not do.
Never describe an SIS set-aside as “expungement”; it leaves a record that can still surface on
background checks and as a sentencing prior.

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Alaska's mechanisms key off completing probation or a short confidentiality window rather than long
post-conviction waits.

Mechanism Trigger / waiting period Authority

SIS set-aside On successful completion of the SIS probation AS 12.55.085(e)
period (clean for the term, often ≈ 1 year+)

Mistaken-identity sealing No fixed wait; proof beyond a reasonable doubt AS 12.62.180(b)

Courtview non-publication 60 days after acquittal or dismissal AS 22.35.030
(acquittal / dismissal)

Suspended entry of judgment On completion; dismissal w/o conviction AS 12.55.078

Juvenile sealing 30 days after 18th birthday / release of AS 47.12.300
jurisdiction; 5 years if charged as adult

Two timing points matter. The SIS set-aside is available only AFTER the person successfully completes the
suspended-imposition probation period without violation — there is no additional multi-year post-completion
wait, but the conviction is not set aside until probation is done and the court acts. The Courtview confidentiality
rule runs on a simple 60-day clock from the acquittal or dismissal and is meant to be automatic; the TF-810
process exists only to correct cases the system failed to remove. A suspended ENTRY of judgment (AS
12.55.078) is generally available only once in a lifetime, absent clear and convincing evidence of a high
likelihood of rehabilitation.

AGENT GUIDANCE
For acquittals/dismissals, the relief should be automatic at 60 days — have the user check Courtview first
and only file TF-810 if the case is still showing.
For an SIS, the set-aside comes at successful completion of probation; confirm probation is fully
complete before expecting the court to set the conviction aside.

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying offenses / what cannot be cleared
sourceSections.text:
The biggest limitation is structural: no general expungement exists, so an ordinary accurate adult conviction
generally cannot be cleared at all. Within the narrow mechanisms, additional exclusions apply.

Excluded from SIS / set-aside (AS 12.55.085(f))
• Serious and violent offenses — e.g., murder and other homicide offenses, and offenses where an SIS is
statutorily barred.
• Sexual offenses and offenses against children, as enumerated.
• Certain crimes involving domestic violence and other offenses specifically listed in AS 12.55.085(f).
• Offenses with a mandatory minimum sentence that cannot be suspended.

Limits across the mechanisms
• Set-aside is not erasure. Even when granted, the conviction remains on the record, can be a sentencing prior
(AS 12.55.155(c)(8)), and may support licensing denials.
• Sealing (12.62.180) is essentially limited to mistaken identity / false accusation proven beyond a
reasonable doubt — not a route for clearing valid records.
• Courtview non-publication only hides the case from the public website — law enforcement and agencies
retain the record, and it is limited to acquittals/dismissals.
• Suspended entry of judgment (12.55.078) is typically once per lifetime and has its own excluded-offense
list.
• Out-of-state and federal records are not reachable by Alaska's mechanisms.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The SIS / set-aside exclusion list (AS 12.55.085(f)) and the suspended-entry-of-judgment
exclusions (AS 12.55.078(f)) are specific and have been amended; the reform bills (HB 34, HB 203)
would, if enacted, add genuine expungement/destruction of set-aside records and change this
picture substantially. The agent must verify the current statutes and whether any reform has
become law before advising — and must not assume expungement exists.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Screen SIS eligibility against AS 12.55.085(f) — homicide, sex offenses, and certain DV and
mandatory-minimum offenses are out.
Be explicit about the ceiling: for a valid adult conviction that isn't SIS-eligible and isn't a mistaken-identity
case, Alaska currently offers no clearing beyond a governor's pardon.

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Disposition drives which (if any) mechanism applies. This table is the agent's primary routing logic for Alaska.

Case outcome Mechanism Effect Authority

Acquittal (all charges) Courtview non-pub. Off public site at 60 days AS 22.35.030

All charges dismissed Courtview non-pub. Off public site at 60 days AS 22.35.030

Dismissed after suspended Courtview non-pub. Off public site; no conviction 12.55.078 / 22.35.030
entry of judgment

Conviction w/ SIS, probation Set-aside Conviction set aside (NOT erased) AS 12.55.085(e)
completed

Record from mistaken identity Sealing Sealed; may deny existence AS 12.62.180(b)
/ false accusation

Juvenile adjudication Juvenile sealing Sealed at 18 / release AS 47.12.300

Ordinary adult conviction — No clearing; pardon only —
(no SIS, accurate)

Effect of each remedy differs sharply. A sealed record (12.62.180) is confidential and the person may deny its
existence and the underlying arrest/charge/conviction. A set-aside conviction (12.55.085) is NOT sealed: it
remains a record, can still appear on background checks, may be used as a sentencing aggravator, and the
Alaska courts have described the post-SIS dismissal as “in the nature of a pardon, not a declaration of
innocence.” Courtview non-publication (22.35.030) keeps an acquittal/dismissal off the public website but
does not destroy the record held by agencies. The agent must convey these differences precisely.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Match remedy to disposition: acquittal/dismissal → Courtview non-publication (verify it happened); SIS
conviction → set-aside (explain it's not erasure); mistaken identity → sealing.
Only a sealed record (or a non-public Courtview entry) drops off public view; a set-aside conviction does
not, so don't overpromise.

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
There is no “petition for expungement” in Alaska. The relevant filings are the Alaska Court System (TF) forms
and statutory requests.

Form / filing Use

Form TF-810 Request to exclude a case from Courtview (Admin. Rule 40 / AS 22.35.030) — acquittals, dismissals, completed SIS set-asid

Form TF-805 Request to remove only the person's NAME from the public index

Form TF-800 Request asking a judge to seal or make confidential a still-public case or document

SIS / set-aside Motion in the criminal case for set-aside on completion of the SIS (AS 12.55.085(e)); the court issues a certificate

AS 12.62.180(b) request Written request to the agency head to seal mistaken-identity / false-accusation records

For an acquittal or dismissal that is still appearing on Courtview, Form TF-810 is the workhorse: it asks the
court to exclude the case from the public index, and the petitioner shows that 60 days have passed since the
acquittal or dismissal and attaches proof. For a conviction resolved through an SIS, the set-aside is sought by
motion in the underlying criminal case once probation is complete; the court issues a certificate that the person
can provide to the Department of Public Safety and the clerk. Mistaken-identity sealing under AS 12.62.180(b)
is a written request to the agency that holds the record, not a court petition.

AGENT GUIDANCE
For acquittals/dismissals use TF-810 (whole case), TF-805 (name only), or TF-800 (ask a judge to seal)
— pick the one that matches the need. There is no expungement petition.
For an SIS, the set-aside is a motion in the existing criminal case; for mistaken identity, it's a written
sealing request to the record-holding agency.

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Identify the disposition. Acquittal/dismissal, SIS conviction, mistaken-identity record, or juvenile — this
determines the mechanism (or that none applies).

2 Check Courtview first. For an acquittal or dismissal, look up the case; if 60 days have passed and it is no
longer on the public index, no filing is needed.

3 If still public (acquittal/dismissal): complete Form TF-810 (or TF-805 / TF-800 as appropriate), attach
proof of the acquittal/dismissal and that 60 days have elapsed, and file with the trial court where the case was
filed.

4 If an SIS conviction: after successfully completing probation, file a motion in the criminal case asking the
court to set aside the conviction under AS 12.55.085(e); obtain the court's certificate and provide copies to the
Department of Public Safety and the clerk.
5 If mistaken identity / false accusation: submit a written request under AS 12.62.180(b) to the head of the
agency that maintains the record, with evidence establishing the error beyond a reasonable doubt.

6 If juvenile: confirm the record is sealed under AS 47.12.300; if not, raise it with the juvenile court.
7 If none fits: advise that Alaska provides no general clearing for the conviction and that a governor's pardon is
the only avenue; provide accurate expectations rather than a false path.

AGENT GUIDANCE
File TF-810 with the trial court that handled the case; it's a short request with proof attached, not an
adversarial petition.
For an SIS set-aside, the motion goes into the EXISTING criminal case once probation is complete —
there's no separate new action.

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• No general expungement. The defining feature of Alaska — Journey v. State confirms the set-aside law
does not authorize expunging a record.
• Set-aside ≠ sealing. An SIS set-aside leaves the record intact and visible on background checks; it is “in the
nature of a pardon, not a declaration of innocence.”
• Automatic Courtview removal. Acquittals and dismissals come off the public website at 60 days by rule —
the relief is automatic, with TF-810 only as a backstop.
• Set-aside cases also leave Courtview. A completed SIS set-aside where all other charges resolved favorably
is also removed from the public index under Admin. Rule 40(a)(13).
• Marijuana and minor-alcohol carve-outs. Admin. Rule 40 also keeps certain old small-amount-marijuana
convictions and some under-21 alcohol offenses off the public index.
• Sealing is essentially mistaken-identity only. AS 12.62.180(b) is not a general remedy.
• Suspended entry of judgment (12.55.078) avoids a conviction entirely but is usually a once-per-lifetime
option.
• Reform pending. HB 34 / HB 203 would add real expungement/destruction of set-aside records — track
whether either becomes law.

AGENT GUIDANCE
The single most important thing to get right in Alaska is not overpromising: distinguish non-publication
and set-aside (record persists) from true sealing/expungement (which generally doesn't exist here).
Watch the reform bills — if HB 34 or HB 203 is enacted, the set-aside-to-expungement picture changes
and this reference will need updating.

sourceSections.text:
Because Alaska has no expungement petition, the examples below show the actual filings used. Names, dates,
and identifiers are fictional and illustrative.

Example A — Form TF-810 (exclude an acquittal/dismissal from Courtview)
ALASKA COURT SYSTEM — TRIAL COURT, THIRD JUDICIAL DISTRICT
AT ANCHORAGE
STATE OF ALASKA v. ANDRE L. CARTER Case No. 3AN-23-01234CR

REQUEST TO EXCLUDE CASE FROM COURTVIEW
(Admin. Rule 40(a) / AS 22.35.030) — Form TF-810
1. I am the defendant in the case above.

sourceSections.title: SET-ASIDE AFTER SIS (AS 12.55.085(e)) — MOTION IN THE CASE:
sourceSections.text:
'Defendant received a Suspended Imposition of Sentence and has
successfully completed probation. Defendant moves the Court to
SET ASIDE the conviction under AS 12.55.085(e) and to issue a
certificate.' (NOTE: this does NOT seal or expunge; the record
remains and may appear on background checks.)

sourceSections.title: SEALING — MISTAKEN IDENTITY / FALSE ACCUSATION (AS 12.62.180(b)):
sourceSections.text:
A written request to the head of the agency holding the record,
showing beyond a reasonable doubt that the record resulted from
mistaken identity or false accusation. If sealed, the person may
deny the existence of the information.
